key_insight: The paper proposes a modular calculus that separates optimizer geometry from nongeometric mechanisms and characterizes exactly which descent directions are reachable under given constraints.
engineering_relevance: This gives optimizer designers a principled way to trade off metric richness, memory, control, and compute budgets instead of relying on ad hoc comparisons of optimizers.
method: Defines a geometric module as a positive cometric mapping gradients to update directions.
Introduces nongeometric modules for information, memory, step-size control, constraints, noise, target modification, and discretization.
Proves direction-expressivity results, including exact conditions for diagonal and block geometries, and extends them to trajectory-level residual complexity.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports diagnostic prototypes only: a full-metric probe solves deterministic quadratic benchmarks to numerical precision, and a Muon-style PyTorch candidate provides small-scale evidence for auditability.
watch_item: The framework is primarily theoretical and benchmark-language oriented, so the proposed diagnostics and expressivity claims still need broader empirical validation on large-scale training.
paper_title: Geometric--Nongeometric Optimizer Calculus: A Modular Language for Reachable Gradient Methods
